title: Neoliberalism and the Dharma
subtitle: Greed, Generosity, and the Seeds of Change
epigraph: If beings knew, as I knew, the results of giving and sharing, they would not eat without having given.
paragraphs: This evening I thought I'd tackle neoliberalism, save us all, and explore the role of Buddha Dharma in relation to that particular ideology.

Just to remind ourselves, an ideology is a set of beliefs and principles that go on to form a policy. But as an ideology—as an idealism as well—there's always some future goal, some sort of Shangri-La it's heading towards. And every ideology carries within it the seed of its own destruction. In Marxist terms, that seed lies in capitalism. But as we've discovered from the last century, it's true of any ideology: communism, national socialism, even democracy—though democracy at least has this self-correction every so often. And as we've also discovered, it only really works when a society tends to be fairly peaceful with itself. These days, as we see, there's a leaning towards the right again, towards authoritarianism.

The Buddha himself didn't fashion Buddhism. He simply taught a practical path to liberation and the understanding that underlies it. It's really about ethics, and he belonged to a period before writing. There wasn't this philosophy and systems theory and all that—it just didn't exist at that time. The thought was very practical.

Now, the fundamental attitude that drives neoliberalism is greed, acquisitiveness. I'm just quoting here from Oliver Stone's 1987 movie Wall Street. Some of you might have seen it. Gordon Gekko is the antihero, played by Michael Douglas, and he gives this speech: "The point is, ladies and gentlemen, that greed, for lack of a better word, is good. Greed is right. Greed works. Greed clarifies, cuts through and captures the essence of the evolutionary spirit. Greed in all its forms—greed for life, greed for money, for love, knowledge—has marked the upward surge of mankind."

What this attitude does is make you a very special human being. You're out for yourself. It's this very selfish attitude that I should have what I want when I want it. If you remember Frank Sinatra's old song: "I did it my way."

Of course, it's a corruption of what Darwin said. Darwin, and I quote him here: "It is not the strongest of the species that survive, not the most intelligent, but the ones most responsive to change." So this word "fittest" actually means adaptable.

One thing that Jill Westcott points out in her book The Psychological Roots of the Climate Crisis is that the wealthy nurtured the concept of neoliberalism through right-wing think tanks, and they captured the Republicans and eventually the Tories. So that's dear old Margaret Thatcher for us.

This acquisitiveness, this greed, operates in a market. We're all now entrepreneurs in this market, and we all want everything all at once. What it does, of course, is create competition. But it's a competition that is antagonistic—because either you're, at best, my rival, or at worst, you're my direct enemy. So I'm always working against somebody.

Now, you can't say that competition is all bad, because it does create efficiency and inventiveness. In fact, in the worst cases of aggressive competition—war, remember, in the Second World War—jet engines, radar, and the computer were developed. So there's a tremendous irony there.

But the thing is, what neoliberalism presumes is that society is at its best when it services the market. We're all individuals, all working against each other in order to get the biggest piece of the pie we can. The whole process is very antagonistic.

When you look at something like academia, though, where scientists from all over the world might be working on a particular project, they're very happy to share their discoveries with each other. They're not motivated by power or money—they're given a salary. They might covet the Nobel Prize, but it's usually just sheer interest in the scientific investigation. So here we have a collaborative competitiveness, which is a very different atmosphere altogether. I think it's something the Buddha would have liked.

The downside of this, of course, is that if you're not a player in the market, then in a sense you've only yourselves to blame. And in blaming the victims—if you remember going back to the Thatcher period, those from the rest of Europe won't remember this—we had this fellow called Tebbit who told people to get onto their bikes. Like, it's up to you. You've got to get the work. And if you don't get the work, well, that's your problem. You're to blame.

So workers begin to lose their rights, because basically you have to negotiate with your employer on your own. And the competition between workers determines the cost—which goes down and down. This is the gig economy, isn't it? This is what we mean by the race to the bottom.

You can see it clearly in how Uber started in London. Those who wanted to join were in competition with each other. Imagine a parent with children and holidays to pay for, a young woman with a mortgage, and a young fellow who's got no problems whatsoever—he's only about 21. They're all bidding for your ride. If the parent charges 30, the woman charges 25, and he charges 20, then if the parent wants that ride, they've got to come all the way down to 20. And that's what happens. Everything starts moving downwards.

The worst example, of course, is the recent sacking of 800 workers by the P&O ferry line. Interestingly enough, the company was losing 100 million, but at the same time they paid out 270 million to their shareholders. So you can see it's not very fair, is it?

And we all know about this tremendous—well, it comes out to be a lie, doesn't it? This thing about trickle down: eventually the wealth trickles down and everybody's happy. But it doesn't trickle down to the unemployed, to the disabled, to the aged. These people are not players in the market. They're seen as a real drag, draining money away.

The only way to get around that, of course, is to privatise all the social services so that companies can get their 10%. I don't know what's happening in the rest of Europe, but in Britain many GP surgeries are now being taken over by a company called Centene. Even the Daily Mail called it the most avaricious of insurance companies. So it's all bad news, isn't it?

But the big thing is that this competition undermines any sense of common wealth. It's no longer a caring society. You're not actually caring for each other—you're just trying to find your way, make money. Any government or charitable effort that tries to address people's basic physical or material well-being is seen as undermining the entrepreneurial spirit of competition. People have got to get out there and get into it.

And of course, neoliberalism hates governments interfering with markets. So there was this whole business about very few regulations. We know what happened. The banks went berserk. And then who had to step in to save them? Us, the poor ordinary punter.
You can see that this is diametrically opposed to anything the Buddha would teach.

And the wonderful thing is that, of course, people are highly stressed in that sort of system—all the way down from CEOs to the ordinary worker. Now, it seems that the World Economic Forum at Davos, where the rich and the powerful meet to decide how to rule the world, were overjoyed to hear about mindfulness.

Mindfulness would help people with their stress. So they began telling all the workers who were stressed out that they had to take a course in mindfulness and stress reduction. And if you happened to be even more stressed by even more pressure, all you had to do was find a place in the office somewhere, sit there for a while, watch your breath, and it would all disappear—as though stress were entirely a person's internal response to a situation. But as we know, the stress that's put upon you from the outside has a great deal to do with our mental states.

So this wonderful gift that the Buddha gave us—investigating our lives and our being in order to achieve complete liberation from suffering—is now being used to support a system that's doing exactly the opposite. What an irony.

And of course, what the neoliberal system is doing is the work of Māra. Māra is the evil one. If you remember, just before the Buddha's liberation, Māra came and gave him this great doubt. He said, come on, what are you doing? Why are you going through all this pain, this suffering? Why don't you get back to your family, get back to sensual pleasures, back to how it was in the old days before you took up this silly idea?

And of course, the Buddha touches the earth. This is the important thing. He touches the earth and the goddess of the earth arises and says to Māra, no, the Buddha has every right to do this because he has perfected the virtue of generosity. In other words, the Buddha's commitment came from his desire to free everybody from suffering—not just himself.

This is really interesting, because it undermines the neoliberal agenda: the idea that we can actually be like citizens who help each other. If you remember during the lockdown, suddenly neighbours realised they could help themselves. It was unknown. You could actually knock on a neighbour's door and say, how are you doing? And be there to help them out. This was a new discovery, it seems.

Now, just before I carry on with generosity, the question is: what can we personally do? The obvious thing, of course, is to be aware of what we're buying—not to support these satanic factories in Bangladesh and Cambodia and even in China.

But coming back to generosity. I'm sure you've noticed that when you do something out of a sense of care for yourself—like buying a warm jumper—there's always a feeling good about that. You feel good about yourself. You're caring for yourself. But have you noticed that when you care for somebody else, when you look after somebody else, when you buy something for somebody, when you give something to somebody or spend time with them, the joy that comes from it is much more long lasting?

It's that shift from me towards we where we can begin to find a different sort of economic system. Of course, that sounds a bit glib—me to we and all that. But basically, it's that sense of bringing back community.

I would just like to end with this lovely quote from the Buddha: 'If beings knew, as I know, the results of giving and sharing, they would not eat without having given, nor would the stain of miserliness overcome their minds. Even if it were their last bite, their last mouthful, they would not eat without having shared, if there were someone to receive the gift. But because beings do not know this, as I know, the results of giving and sharing, they eat without having given and the stain of miserliness overcomes their minds.'

So it's possible that we can have a life of joyfulness—a shared joy with people around us—and get away from this very tight sense of the little self, like a little castle that has to defend itself against everybody.

All we can hope for is a change of attitude. You'll get politicians saying, oh, what we need is better jobs and better housing. But that's all within the system of neoliberalism. If we're going to move towards something much more just, much more equitable, much more kind, much more caring, it's that fundamental attitude that has to change.
And let's hope that when it does happen—because the seeds of destruction are within capitalism itself—let's hope it's not another ideology, because that again will create problems.

What the Buddha is saying is this: if you ground yourself in the right attitude, and you recognise that times and situations are in constant flux, then you can always come at a particular situation not from some preset view and opinion, but from this attitude of compassion, of sympathetic joy, of caring, of love, for heaven's sake.

Anyway, I hope my words have been of some assistance, that they haven't caused even greater confusion, and that by your commitment to the good life of compassion and joy, you will be liberated from all suffering sooner rather than later.
